narration_segments.text: It starts in the stomach. Before the word yes leaves the mouth — before the smile forms on the face, before the nod of agreement, before the body leans forward in that automatic gesture of accommodation that has become so habitual it feels like personality rather than pattern — the stomach sends its signal. A drop. A twist. A slow churning that begins deep in the abdomen, rises toward the throat, and dies there, swallowed back down because the social moment does not seem to allow the time or the space or the permission for refusal.

Her name is Nenet. Nenet is thirty-eight years old. She is the middle child of five — sandwiched between two older siblings who were loud enough to demand attention and two younger siblings who were small enough to receive it automatically. She teaches secondary school history. She is the person everyone calls when something needs doing — the colleague who covers extra shifts without being asked twice, the sister who organizes every family gathering, the friend who answers the phone at eleven on a Tuesday night because someone needs to talk and Nenet has never, not once, let a call go to voicemail. Nenet's calendar has no white space. Not one square of empty time in any given week. Every evening is claimed by some commitment. Every weekend is spoken for before it arrives. She volunteers at the community literacy center on Tuesdays. She drives her elderly neighbor, Mrs. Farouk, to medical appointments on Thursdays. She mentors two first-year teachers at her school, both of whom message her at all hours with questions they could answer themselves with five minutes of independent thought but choose not to because Nenet always answers and Nenet never says: look it up yourself.

These practices work with the body's boundary intelligence — the stomach, the jaw, the shoulders — rather than with the mind's social calculations. They are physical before they are verbal. They are felt before they are spoken. The body must learn to trust its own alarm again before the mouth can speak its truth. PRACTICE ONE: THE THREE-SECOND PAUSE This is the foundation practice. It changes the entire architecture of response, and it requires almost nothing — three seconds of attention and the willingness to feel. When someone makes a request — any request, from the trivial to the significant, from a text message to a face-to-face conversation — do not answer immediately. Pause for three seconds. Place one hand on the stomach if possible. Feel what is there. The stomach will respond. It always responds. It has been responding for the entire span of a life.

The compressed teaching. Receive it in the body, where truth is weighed. Let it land in the stomach, where every honest thing is felt before it is known. A boundary is not a punishment. A boundary is not a wall. A boundary is not a rejection of love or a failure of generosity or a betrayal of the people who have come to depend on a version of you that was never the real one. A boundary is alignment. The river does not punish the water by having banks.
